sections.heading: What is Android Studio?
sections.content: Android Studio is the official Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for Android development, built by Google on top of IntelliJ IDEA. It provides everything you need to build, test, debug, and publish Android apps — including a code editor, layout designer, emulator, profiler, and build tools — all in one place.

sections.heading: Why Use Android Studio?
items: Official IDE recommended by Google for Android development
Intelligent code editor with Kotlin & Java support
Built-in Android Emulator for testing without a physical device
Visual Layout Editor to design UIs with drag-and-drop
Integrated Gradle build system for dependency management
Real-time Logcat for debugging and monitoring logs
Profiler tools for CPU, memory, and network performance
Direct APK/AAB generation and Google Play publishing support

sections.heading: Installing Android Studio
sections.content: Download Android Studio from https://developer.android.com/studio. Run the installer and follow the setup wizard. During setup, Android Studio will automatically install the Android SDK, Android Emulator, and required SDK tools. Once installed, launch Android Studio and complete the first-time setup to download the latest SDK components.

sections.heading: Key Windows & Panels in Android Studio
items: Project Panel – Shows your project files and folder structure on the left side
Editor Window – Central area where you write and edit code or XML layouts
Layout Editor – Visual designer for XML UI files with a live preview
Logcat – Displays real-time device/emulator logs for debugging
Terminal – Built-in terminal for running ADB and Gradle commands
Build Output – Shows Gradle build results and any compilation errors
Device Manager – Manage and launch Android Virtual Devices (AVDs)
Profiler – Monitor CPU, memory, energy, and network usage in real time

sections.heading: Creating Your First Android Project
sections.content: To create a new project: open Android Studio → click 'New Project' → select a template (e.g., 'Empty Activity') → configure your project name, package name, save location, language (Kotlin), and minimum SDK → click 'Finish'. Android Studio will generate a starter project with all necessary files and open it in the editor.

rows.description: Human-readable name of your app (e.g., MyFirstApp)
Unique identifier for your app (e.g., com.example.myfirstapp)
Directory on your computer where the project will be saved
Lowest Android version your app will support (e.g., API 24 / Android 7.0)
Kotlin DSL (recommended) or Groovy DSL for Gradle scripts

sections.heading: Understanding the Project Structure
items: app/ – Main module containing all your app's source code and resources
app/src/main/java/ – Kotlin/Java source files (Activities, ViewModels, etc.)
app/src/main/res/ – All resource files: layouts, drawables, strings, themes
app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml – App configuration, permissions, and component declarations
app/src/test/ – Unit test files (run on JVM, no device needed)
app/src/androidTest/ – Instrumented test files (run on device/emulator)
Gradle Scripts/ – build.gradle files for project and app-level build config
.gradle/ & .idea/ – Auto-generated IDE and build cache files (do not edit manually)

rows.purpose: Entry point of the app; the first screen displayed to the user
XML layout file defining the UI of MainActivity
Declares app components, permissions, and app metadata
App-level build config: dependencies, SDK versions, plugins
Project-level build config: repositories and global plugins
Stores all text strings used in the app for easy localization
Defines the visual theme, colors, and styles for the app
Project-wide Gradle settings like JVM args and AndroidX flags

sections.heading: The res/ Directory in Detail
sections.content: The res/ (resources) directory is where all non-code assets live. Android separates resources from code to support different screen sizes, languages, and configurations automatically. Each subfolder has a specific purpose, and Android selects the appropriate resource at runtime based on the device configuration.

rows.purpose: XML files that define the UI structure of screens and components
Images, icons, shapes, and vector XML drawables
App launcher icons in different resolutions (hdpi, xhdpi, xxhdpi, etc.)
All user-facing text strings for easy localization
Color hex values used throughout the app
XML files defining action bar and context menus
XML animation definitions for view transitions
Raw files like audio, JSON, or other assets accessed via R.raw
Misc XML config files (e.g., network security config, backup rules)

sections.heading: Understanding the Gradle Build System
items: Gradle is the build system Android Studio uses to compile, link, and package your app
The project-level build.gradle defines repositories and plugins available to all modules
The app-level build.gradle defines dependencies, compileSdk, minSdk, targetSdk, and versionCode
dependencies {} block is where you add third-party libraries like Retrofit, Room, Hilt
Sync Project with Gradle Files (elephant icon) must be clicked after any build.gradle change
Gradle generates the R.java/R.kt file mapping all resources to integer IDs for use in code

rows.description: The Android API level to compile your app against (e.g., 35)
The minimum Android API level the app supports (e.g., 24)
The Android API level the app is optimized and tested for
Internal integer version number, incremented for each release
Human-readable version string shown to users (e.g., "1.0.0")
Block listing all external libraries your app depends on

sections.heading: Running Your App on an Emulator
items: Open Device Manager from the toolbar or Tools menu
Click 'Create Device' and choose a hardware profile (e.g., Pixel 6)
Select a system image (download a recent API level like API 34)
Click 'Finish' to create the Android Virtual Device (AVD)
Press the green Run button (▶) or Shift+F10 to build and launch the app
The emulator will boot and your app will be installed and opened automatically
Use the emulator toolbar to simulate rotation, GPS, battery, and network conditions

rows.shortcut: Ctrl + Alt + L (Win) / Cmd + Alt + L (Mac)
Ctrl + Shift + F (Win) / Cmd + Shift + F (Mac)

rows.action: Select next occurrence of current word

sections.heading: Android Studio Tips for Beginners
items: Always sync Gradle after modifying build.gradle to avoid build errors
Use 'Android' view in the Project panel (not 'Project' view) for a cleaner file tree
Enable 'Auto Import' in Settings → Editor → General → Auto Import for Kotlin
Use Logcat filters (by tag or package) to reduce noise when debugging
Invalidate Caches (File → Invalidate Caches) if you see unexplained red errors
Keep Android Studio and SDK tools updated via the SDK Manager for the latest features

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Android Studio is the cornerstone of Android development. Understanding its layout, the project structure it generates, and how Gradle ties everything together will make you productive from the start. Take time to explore the IDE panels, create a sample project, and run it on the emulator before moving on to building real app features.
